findings.description: Off-by-one: `len(hits) <= self.limit` admits the request when there are already `limit` timestamps in the window (e.g. 100 <= 100 appends a 101st), so the limiter permits `limit + 1` requests per window instead of `limit` — the configured limit is silently exceeded by one for every client every window.

No concurrency control: `check` does a non-atomic read-modify-write (prune via `pop(0)`, length test, then `append`) on shared mutable `_hits` state, so on a concurrent 'public API gateway' two requests can both observe the count as under the limit and both be admitted, over-admitting beyond even the off-by-one and corrupting the per-key list under racing pop/append.

`remaining` can be returned negative: the over-permitting branch computes `self.limit - len(hits)` after appending, yielding -1 on the (limit+1)-th allowed request, leaking a negative value through the `Decision.remaining` int contract that callers typically surface as an `X-RateLimit-Remaining` header.

`sweep` iterates `self._hits.items()` and deletes keys based on that snapshot of membership; if `check`/`reset`/`current_usage` mutate `_hits` (add or remove a key) concurrently during the sweep, Python raises `RuntimeError: dictionary changed size during iteration`, crashing the background memory-bounding task.

`current_usage` reads `self._hits[key]` on a `defaultdict`, which has the side effect of creating and persisting a new empty bucket for any key queried — a nominally read-only method mutating state and contributing to unbounded `_hits` growth when called for many distinct/never-seen clients (e.g. monitoring/probing).

`_client_key` trusts the first hop of the client-supplied `X-Forwarded-For` header with no allowlist/validation, so a client can spoof a unique value per request to obtain a fresh bucket each time (rate-limit bypass) and simultaneously drive unbounded-cardinality `_hits` growth (memory exhaustion); primarily a security finding — deferring exploit detail to critic-security, noted here for its logic/memory-growth dimension.
